buggy_code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

df = pd.read_csv('features.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

X = df.drop('target', axis=1).values
y = df['target'].values

# Train on all data
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100)
model.fit(X, y)

# Predict on SAME data used for training — in-sample predictions
df['predicted'] = model.predict(X)

# Compute 'performance' on in-sample predictions
df['strategy_return'] = df['predicted'] * df['returns']
print(f'Sharpe: {df["strategy_return"].mean() / df["strategy_return"].std() * 252**0.5:.2f}')
clean_code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

df = pd.read_csv('features.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Chronological split — never predict on training period
split = int(len(df) * 0.7)
train_df = df.iloc[:split]
test_df = df.iloc[split:]

X_train = train_df.drop('target', axis=1).values
y_train = train_df['target'].values
X_test = test_df.drop('target', axis=1).values

model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Only evaluate on unseen test period
test_df['predicted'] = model.predict(X_test)
test_df['strategy_return'] = test_df['predicted'] * test_df['returns']
print(f'Out-of-sample Sharpe: {test_df["strategy_return"].mean() / test_df["strategy_return"].std() * 252**0.5:.2f}')
description: Model trained and evaluated on identical data — in-sample performance reported as strategy performance.
explanation: A RandomForest trained on data and then predicted on that same data shows near-perfect performance because the model has memorized the training data. This is pure in-sample overfitting reported as strategy performance. The real out-of-sample performance is typically dramatically worse.
suggested_fix: Always evaluate on a held-out test period that the model was never trained on. Use chronological splitting.

issues.title: Model evaluated on training data produces inflated in-sample performance
Model evaluated on training data produces inflated in-sample performance
